summary:
Large-scale communal grain storage facilities — granaries — became a defining feature of early urban economies in the Near East during the fourth millennium BCE. At sites associated with the [[event:1617|Uruk period]], substantial storage structures have been identified that could hold significant quantities of barley and wheat beyond the needs of individual households. These facilities imply administrative systems for receiving, recording, and redistributing agricultural surpluses, and they are closely linked to the emergence of [[event:1630|accounting systems]] and early writing as tools for tracking quantities and transactions.

Granary complexes have also been identified at early [[event:1680|Indus Valley Civilization]] sites including [[event:1682|Harappa]] and [[event:1681|Mohenjo-daro]], where large platforms and structures interpreted as granaries suggest centralized storage and redistribution of agricultural goods. The control of grain surpluses was a fundamental mechanism of early state power: it allowed authorities to feed specialist workers, sustain armies, provision temples, and manage food supply during droughts and poor harvests. The granary thus represents an institutional as much as a technological innovation in the history of civilization.

read_more_links.title: Food Storage in the Ancient World - Britannica
